item.title: Mod Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Xanadu_House_Exterior_(2004).jpg

Image Source, CC BY-SA 2.0.

item.title: Re: New Ideas
item.content:encoded:
Take no offense Steaming but I think your ideas would… you know, ruin the spirit of the scip, it's good as it is, and I'm not a fan of fixing things that aren't broken.

The author is the one that has to make the call, though.

item.title: New Ideas
item.content:encoded:
This SCP paints a solid picture that is a little small but doesn't have any rough edges either. It's a good start. If I were to suggest expanding on this, I'd take it one of 2 ways:

  1. Drop hints that the "world of tomorrow" isn't perfect (perhaps by the researcher picking up a stray newspaper). Like, if this house is being advertised as a great way to avoid the zombie plagues that infest the big cities.
  2. What if this house and it's inhabitants are intentionally projected to our dimension from another? Like, if an arrogant religion in another universe were trying to show us savages that there's a better way of life. When they try to incorporate our new technology, they don't know what to make of it. It confuses the actors so they get unhappy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh. Upvoted for the idea, but it is a bit bland.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the house has a kitchen stocked with food pills that change into three-course meals upon hydration, miniature robots that perform routine cleaning and maintenance, and a garage containing a car capable of flight

This is very Back to the Future 2-ish, no?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, the future of mankind is gonna be awesome, just not this one.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I highly approve of how the transition from the "bold, bright future" science-fiction of the 1950s towards darker, dystopian themes of the end of the century is allegorically represented by introduction of new items having a negative effect on the house's inhabitants.

…what?

Seriously though, I found most of the article okay but the last paragraph has done it for me and convinced me to upvote. Well done sir-or-madam.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What about SCP-1122-3 and -4? Won't somebody please think of SCP-1122-4 and -4?!

Utterly loved this one; fun and quirky and then God damn it humanity you're breaking all the nice things again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I ran into this photo gallery just now and was immediately reminded of this article. That picture at the dinner table especially…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How did I never see this. The note at the end makes me sad and contrasts nicely with the basic trope. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't the containment section have something about a cover story for when turning away curious people?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: nice

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So sad…and it's not even real!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Done. Feel free to revert anything you don't like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like an asshole now and I don't even have anything to do with the article. Excellent hook.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this until the Jetsons showed up. At that point, it felt more derivative than anything else, even if the item-addition property is interesting on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have zero issues with this SCP. Amazing work for your first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, the idea is solid. Couple of things that you could add if you wanted to: do they change cloths often, if at all? What does the family do, other then watch TV, eat pills and fly a car to nowhere. And when asked, will any of the family members exit the house, they might turn into manequin (God that word) much like what you would see in a typical ''Family of the future'' ride. Definitely have my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Closeup of Anderson Cooper furrowing his brow and explaining that video games cause violence and obesity. The camera pans over the children of the household who are now blobby, stubby armed people waving knives.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can help but wonder what would happen if we introduced a laptop and a current-gen gaming system to the house.

Edit: Also, +1 for you sir. Being that this is your first article, you knocked it out of the park.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There was some grammar that was hard to get through.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one and it's a good first one as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really love this idea. Perfect blend of weirdness with just a touch of tragedy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the interaction between the idealistic zeerusty image of the future and the reality of the modern age. I especially like the line about how things "should have been better". I think it taps into that part of us that wanted our jetpacks and flying cars.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sounds good. Go ahead. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a darn good SCP concept. The tone has a few minor issues (items that "refuse to function" outside the house, for instance), but overall it's good work — especially for a first try! No vote from me yet, but fix the tone and you'll have my +1.

If you like, I'd be happy to go through and tweak the tone for you. You have only to ask.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ack, sorry! I accidentally hit "edit" when I was reading this on my mobile, and didn't realize I had done so until later. Please accept my apology, and now that I see you removed that addendum, you can also accept my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed that. Thanks for the tip!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this, but like others have said, the tone seems off to me in some areas. I have the same problem though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually really like this one; it's cute, with just the right amount of "what the heck", and a splash of "aw hell we're breaking things by just being around them again, aren't we?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The tone is a little off, it reads a little more casually than it should. The problems are minor, though, and can be easily remedied. Like, "real ones like Martin Luther King, Jr." line would sound much more clinical if it said "real individuals" or "historical figures".

That said, this is a fascinating article. An interesting look at the road we could have taken, but never did. Good stuff, man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're right about the coming decades part, at least. I'll definitely fix that as soon as I can.

My writing in general has that problem, I think. Whenever I read my own stuff it all feels off to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a general thing, like you mention miniature robots and "to demonstrate what life may be like in the coming decades" and I just can't help but feeling there is something 'off'.

It could just be all in my head of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gotcha. I'll fix that as soon as Roget's done editing it (I assume he's doing some proof-reading).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. Can you tell me what's wrong with the tone, exactly, so I can fix it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another thing; I really don't see any particular reason to redact the ages. Maybe you could make it vague like, "appears to be approximately 30 years of age" or something like that, but the blackboxes just feel awkward.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much better, look into getting your tone improved, and this will be a solid article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All right, I removed the addendum and the part about the amnesiacs. I might add an interview log later if I can think of a good way to make it interesting.

I was going to add a test log where more and more technology gets introduced (a VHS tape, an NES, the microwave oven, etc.), but the guide for writing an SCP said no repetitive test logs, and I guess we didn't need a bunch of different variations on "They get something new…and they hate it!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Couple grammar mistakes aside, seems pretty solid to me. It's a neat concept and a good execution; I'd love to see some of those interviews the researchers have had with the family. Upvote, although I'd ditch the Addendum if I were you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh God. I changed "song" to "son." Really sorry about that. I guess my SCP career's not off to the best start.

I guess you're right about the amnesiac part not being necessary. I'll take that out in a sec.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I rather like this twist on the "Family of Tomorrow". Also, I love you for doing image uploads and links directly.

<3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty good. Kill the addendum with fire, though.

The future ain't what it used to be, huh?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Your tone feels off and the concept is pretty boring to me, I also don't get why anyone who even sees the house must be amnesiaced.

a song (SCP-1122-4) approximately █ years of age.

Is my favourite spelling mistake I've seen in a while.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Needs proofreading (the family has a song, age whatever?) and the note at the end should probably go. Otherwise, I like it.

item.title: SCP-1122
item.content:encoded: Here it goes, my first ever SCP. Let me know what you think.
